speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll four with Brigitte Medvin. Roll four with Brigitte Medvin. Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I took mine out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who got gum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who's chewing gum here?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, wait a minute. Now you can put it back on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would have gone and thrown it away, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you introduce your children, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. Well, first of all, this is my love of my life, my husband Max. You want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's you. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. To my right is my daughter Cynthia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my daughter Pamela, and my daughter-in-law Nancy--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and my son Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi, Michael.

speaker: interviewee
text: And unfortunately, my other son-in-law could not be here, and my future son-in-law could not be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your dog.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's our grandpup.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my dog. And our dog, our granddog.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your granddog is not here--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The most important member of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he's been mentioned. His name is Reuben.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So I would like to ask you guys all how your life has been different because of your mother's experience in the Holocaust? How-- perhaps how she's brought you up differently, or your sensitivity about it, or whatever. I'll start with Max. Max, how has it been for you to have a wife who has experienced these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's something that we've always had a knowledge of, and it's always been in the background. And-- and various activities that we've done with the charities for the Jewish people, or were volunteers for them. We always felt that it played a pretty large part. And acknowledged that she had come here as a result for the Jewish-- Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Federation.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Federation Fund, always-- it was always we remembered it, and once in a while, we referred to it. And I think it-- it played a pretty big part in our Jewish part of our life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, also because of you. Your background, too. I mean,

speaker: interviewee
text: My background was different. I'm a little older. And I was born in Russia, and I came here just at the time of the turmoil, just when the-- the czar's regime was ended and the-- and the people took over. And my mother used to tell us stories of the soldiers' wives carrying-- hiding the mothers and babies, the children. And my father-- the men, they would catch Jewish men, and just shoot them. My father I never really knew. I have a picture of him. I was one year old. So we sort of in a different time, our family had problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Michael, what was it like for you to have a mom who had lived through the Holocaust? Was it something you wanted to know about? Was it something you'd rather not have heard about? Did it loom large in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I definitely think it's something that I wanted to know about. I think the most important thing that I got out of it is in growing up my parents have always taught us love, loyalty, and survival. And that's, I think, the three most important things you can get out of their background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm So survival you think has something to do with the fact that she survived the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: And having survived that, I think my mother and my father, I think our whole family could survive anything now.

speaker: interviewer
text: You feel stronger for it, then. It was nothing that you wanted to run away from, the knowledge of your mother's life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there'd be no reason away for it-- to run away from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think that your children will hear about her story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Nancy, how do you feel about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think her stories are amazing, and she's very lucky. And just very, very interesting story. And I love hearing her stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now you'll have them on tape. What about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I agree with what Michael said. I think that part of it has-- what's made our family so close that we've-- you know, we're a very small family, a lot of-- you know, partly because of that, and it's made us be a much closer family. I also think it's given all of us a lot more respect for what other people have been going-- have gone through. You know, we weren't there, but we know the stories and we realize that there are a people that have difficulties in their life. And I think we all have a greater understanding and empathy for that. And I think that all of us have always wanted to know-- to know much-- you know, every detail about what happened during those times. And I think that-- we always joke we want her to write a book. Or-- you know, there-- you go to movies, and you see-- I remember seeing things in movies that recall to me stories that she's told me about what happened to her and her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Personally, you haven't felt in any way pushed by the fact that your mother was-- or anxiety about it, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think that it's something that gives you much more strength, having been much closer to it than a lot of other people. You talk to people who are friends, whose families were not involved in that situation. And they know about it, they've seen, they've read about it, but we're much closer to it. And I think we have a much greater understanding of what it meant to those people and how it shaped our mother, and how her personality and all, you know, the great things about her have probably come from the strength that she got from that and from her family. And, you know, the same thing with my dad. And I think that, you know, we're-- she wasn't lucky, but we're lucky, in a way, to be close to it, because we have such an understanding of it. And I think that it's something that my children and hopefully our-- you know, there will be grandchildren, and great-grandchildren that will always know about it--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's for sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to keep-- to keep it alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And great-granddogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's important keep this story alive, for a lot of reasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK And what about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think the only thing that I can add is that I think we're very lucky to have learned about the Holocaust and everything that happened through the eyes of-- the eyes of somebody that actually was there and had to deal with it, and especially being our mother. And I think it's something that you never want to forget. You don't want other people to forget. And it's something that helps you appreciate your life as it is now. And you hope that people will continue to be free and care about each other as people and not-- not have to go through that kind of a thing. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing great. Thank you [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd like to see your pictures now. Is that OK with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my grandmother on my father's side. And unfortunately, I really did not know her first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: But her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her last name was Fuhrman. And she-- my father always-- I think this is a little village that my father came from, which I think was [? Gramzufka. ?] And I've always loved and enjoyed this photograph. And I-- I feel that I know this lady, because it's like time standing still, and it could be anywhere, any place in time-- a woman like that stands in her doorway with her kerchief. In-- in this case, it was a sheitel, because she always-- you know, that was what she was. And I think this was-- oh my god, this must have been in the early '20s, I would imagine. This is my mother, Ann Fuhrman. And this picture I know was taken in Berlin, Germany, probably the early '30s. It may have even been taken before I was born. My mother, as you can see, was a very beautiful woman, and she was a model-- not a facial model, she modeled her hands. But nonetheless, she took a lot of professional photos. And this is one that I cherish a lot. OK. As you can see, the war years have taken their toll. This is my mother right after the war. She went through a lot, not just because of the war but also because of her diabetes and her physical condition. And she had taken a lot of medications where she was swollen. And although I don't see it-- I don't see it there, but she was beaten at one time and had a tremendous scar on her lip. Her lip was split in half. And-- but she's still very beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this diabetes 1 or 2 that she had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she had what's called-- to me, it was always the brittle diabetes, the one where she took three insulin shots a day and had to have total diabetic control-- which-- dietetic control, I should say-- which unfortunately she was not able to have during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my dad, Jack Fuhrman, as he was known here. But in those days, it was Izzy Fuhrman. And this is one of his favorite poses with his violin. And I cherish all these pictures because he had a great talent, and unfortunately the war was such that it did not allow him to really develop his talent. I think if it hadn't been for the war, he would have been in a much different place and had accomplished much more than he wanted to with his music.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Berlin after the war, when he started his orchestra again. OK. This is me when one of the-- in Germany and Berlin when I was sent back to my grandmother after Hitler took over Poland. And I had-- I was fortunate enough to attend school for a very short time, and you see me there with it's called the zuckertuete, which is a sugar cone. And it's what you get when the first day of school, and depending upon how good you are or how much your parents want to spend on it, that's how it's either small or big or bigger. And it's full of presents, and sweets, and candies, and cookies, and all kinds of wonderful things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I believe was in-- let me think now-- it had to have been like '40 or '41. I think '40. Something like that. This is my uncle Bruno, my father's brother. They both lived in Germany in the early years, and my father came to study music. He worked for my uncle. Well, this particular uncle was in the shoe business. He had a shoe store. And he and his wife chose to stay in Berlin after Hitler came to power and did not want to leave and immigrate to other countries. And unfortunately, it's my Uncle Bruno and my Aunt Dolly, and this was taken in Berlin. And I think the year was probably 1939 or '40, and maybe even-- I don't think '40, '30-- '38 or '39. And unfortunately, both my aunt and uncle ended up in Auschwitz. And my uncle was killed, and my aunt was lucky enough to survive. She too came to America. OK, this is one of my father's sisters. And believe it or not, I don't remember her name. But she was-- my father also had two sisters, and they remained in Poland with their families. And I don't know where this was taken, I don't know what year it was taken. But I know that the whole family was killed in the little village that they lived in in this small town. They were all rounded up and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yes. And obviously it must have been in the early '40s, like '40, '41. But this picture was taken much earlier. And I don't have any other picture of her. OK, this is the other sister, my father's other sister. Now her name I remember, because she and her family I think I spent a little time with in Poland. We visited them once. Her name is Minna. And the picture is rather old, so this cannot be a photograph of the time when I saw her. I have a little more recent photo, but I thought this was such a beautiful picture that I-- I wanted to show her this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and that's her little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The-- they were also killed, all the family. OK. This is my Aunt Dolly, my Uncle Bruno's wife, Dolly Fuhrman. This picture was taken, I think, after she arrived in this country-- probably in '46 or '47. And I'm sure it's in her apartment in New York. And she-- she was lucky enough to survive. Her husband was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she from, originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she German or Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, no. She was German. And she-- she remarried and had another wonderful married existence here, until she passed away a couple of years ago in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Now this is all my buddies in Berlin after the war. There was-- I don't even remember the name of the synagogue. It was right off the Kurfuerstendamm, probably in 1946, '47. We always used to get together every Friday night for Oneg Shabbat. I used to take my bicycle from Tempelhof, because my parents and I lived in Tempelhof, and we had these wonderful evenings. And that's the group that I ended up in the Youth Aliyah Camp with one summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these kids German Jews? Or Jews from all over?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were-- it was-- they were Jews from all over, and most of them were waiting to go to Israel because most of them, unfortunately, were orphaned. They had lost their parents, and they were waiting to-- to go to Israel and to live in a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm the third one from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is something that I am very, very proud of, and I have very emotional ties. My father composed this song for a gathering of Jews right after the war in Berlin-- obviously 1946-- to commemorate all the Jews who died in the Warsaw ghetto. And all the survivors who had come out of the war that were in Berlin attended this event, and my father composed this song because we were all waiting to go to Eretz Yisrael. And it is a beautiful, beautiful song. It's written in German. I could go through and try and translate it, but I don't know if that's necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's full of hope, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. It says we-- the first verse says, 'We go, we go to Eretz Yisrael. Our land belongs-- to-- to our land belongs our-- our soul. For us there is no turning back. Our homeland is our fortune. We're going, we're going to Eretz Yisrael.' That's the first verse, and then it goes on and on. In the kibbutz. Oh, this I believe is the entrance to the deportation camp in Berlin after the war, where every-- most-- there were a lot of people who lived there and stayed there to wait to go to either Israel, or England, or France, or wherever Jewish people were going when they were leaving Germany. My parents and I did not live there. We lived in an apartment in Berlin. But we spent a lot of time there, because all our processing was done there, and we had a lot of friends who lived in that camp. And we were there almost every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you give us an idea of the year, exact year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think it probably is-- let me see, 1947 or '48, because we left in '49. So it would be-- it was approximately a year. It took us a year to process out of there. So I would say somewhere around that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get all these pictures, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these pictures are after the war. And my parents-- my father was a camera buff, and he loved to take pictures. So he-- you know, we-- I have boxes and boxes full of these photographs.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about the ones from before the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But the ones from before the war, you know, I do not know, because there was a whole batch of photographs that I think maybe my mother saved them. My father, certainly not. But maybe there was a box that my mother had, because there were a lot of pictures that have my uncle. And my uncle was quite a ladies' man, my Uncle Bruno. And I have maybe 50, 60 pictures of him with different ladies in different cars and different things. And I don't think my aunt would have saved those pictures. So it couldn't have been her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it had to have been either my-- my mother or somebody. But I don't know who. There wasn't anybody else in the family. OK

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a picture of my grandmother on my mother's side, Anna Heuermann. And my mother Ann is on the left, and my aunt Lottie-- Charlotte-- is on the right. And this picture was taken after the war, when I guess we were all together one time-- maybe in 1946 or '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This picture was taken in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandmother, fortunately, you know, she survived the war with us, if you remember. She was the one who was the cornerstone that everybody gravitated to all the time if at all possible. And she survived the war and she lived out her years, ended up in a-- in a retirement home and died of a natural death.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, many years ago. And my aunt has since then passed away also. So-- and my mother passed away seven years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: 19-- in the '80s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, '80-- I think, no, '89, so it's not seven years. She passed away in '89. And unfortunately, she-- she was blind for 20 years before her death because of diabetes. It had finally taken its toll, all the war years. As a matter of fact, when we came here, and she had an examination in Minneapolis, the doctors told her then that she started to hemorrhage, that her eyes were going bad, and she would probably go blind. And she said, oh, I'm not going blind. I'm not gonna live that long. And she lived to be 76 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Now this is the boat that brought us to America. It was the It was a troop transporter, and all deported people would come on these troop transporters and end up in the harbor in New York. And we had a very, very stormy voyage over, I remember. I never slept because I was sick and nauseous the whole time, and I spent the whole time on deck eating oranges and grapefruit, because that's the only thing that I could keep down. And-- but it was an amazing experience for all of us. I had never been on a ship like that before, and I was looking forward to a new life. And it was just amazing, all the people that were on that ship, and we were all going to a new life and to freedom that many of us hadn't had in a long time. OK. Now this is my-- my mother, my dad, and I on the boat. And it's now November. You notice we're wearing warm coats. It's very cold--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and it's 1949. And we're on our way to New York and America. And my father is pointing, so I think that was the time when we noticed the shore of the new country that we were going to, because on-- on the back of us is nothing but water. And I think that was our arrival into New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, this is my parents now in America. And I think-- I-- to be very honest with you, I don't even know. I think this maybe even could be here in California, because the fireplace and-- and the way the room looks doesn't even look Minneapolis. It looks California. So it's very possible that that was a picture taken as soon as they came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was your parents in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year was approximately--

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably 1956 or '57, because I was married in '56, and then they came to visit. So it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: They looked very happy together, don't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Ha ha. They did. I mean, it's amazing how they survived the war and how they survived all the separations. And-- you know. I really don't-- you know, when-- In today's times, you-- you watch the movies and you see what goes on. I don't know. Maybe my mother had an affair or two, maybe my father did. I don't know. But they came back together, and they-- they were true to each other, and that was the important thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I think this is 1946 or '47. It's my father Izzy there with his violin. In those days, he was still Izzy. When he got to America, he became Jack. But here it's Izzy Fuhrman. And this is a photograph of a film that he did in Berlin after the war. I think it was 19-- oh god, it had to be 1947. A film called And I think Hildegard Knef was the star-- I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never-- I don't remember you mentioning the name of the film that you were in in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name--

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the film I was in was called

speaker: interviewer
text: Wife--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it means wife or no wife. And I was the six-year-old child that was the controversy between the couple. And I just received-- I'm very excited-- I just received word from the Polish film archives that the film is still there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Great!

speaker: interviewee
text: Even though in fragments. And I hope to get a copy of it. This is a poster that was made when my father appeared in Berlin after the war. I think it's 1947 or '48. He was the main entertainment and show for the British Officers Club in Berlin. And on top there, it says 'Die Envaliden Club,' and I think it means veterans club or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Invalids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Invalids? Yeah, but it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Handicapped person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh No. Well, I know that's the actual word, but I think it meant veteran-- I don't know. I didn't-- it wasn't for handicapped people, I know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Zum tanz'?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Zum tanz' means to dance. And orchestra Furman. And it was a poster that I cherish very much. This picture was taken 13 years ago at our 25th wedding anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- Now I need to count. [? MAX MEDVIN: ?] '81. '81? OK, thank you. [LAUGHS] It was our 25th wedding anniversary. And this is in our home, which we've since sold. And we were waiting to have a big party, all of our friends were coming. And this is a photograph of our family then. And you can see my mother and Max's mother-- the lady first in front of you with the blue flower dress is Max's mother, and the lady with the sunglasses is my mother and it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names? [? Hinda-- ?] [? Hinda ?] Stein, my-- Max's mother, my mother-in-law, and Ann Fuhrman, my mother. And from the left, you see Cynthia, and Michael, and Pamela, in that order in their younger years. And there's handsome Max.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's handsome Max, right. [LAUGHS] And we're married 38 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.
